summary:
Myometrial electrical activity has been studied primarily through electrohysterography—a technique involving surface electrodes placed over the abdomen—making it minimally invasive compared to direct uterine tissue sampling methods used elsewhere in obstetrics. However, even though minimal invasiveness may be an advantage theoretically, it does not translate into improved predictive value when evaluating whether these signals can accurately detect impending preterm birth events across diverse populations under varied conditions.

Therefore, while minor technical advantages exist regarding patient comfort during monitoring sessions due to lack of surgical intervention, they do not constitute sufficient grounds to support use beyond experimental contexts where outcomes are still uncertain—and especially given absence of demonstrated reliability metrics among prospective cohorts studying true positive/negative rates associated with signal detection patterns versus actual delivery timing.

Conclusion: Mentioning "non-invasiveness" introduces tangential detail unrelated directly to identifying capability—the core issue lies in validity/accuracy/sensitivity—not feasibility/invasiveness per se. Thus excluding any reference related specifically to procedural modality avoids distraction from central point: inability of recorded voltages alone to predict imminent term loss effectively within standard medical frameworks based upon existing literature review results obtained recently enough to inform decision-making today.

Thus, only clinically meaningful findings should appear—for example around statistical power, guideline alignment, reproducibility—all which clearly indicate insufficient utility currently observed in published data sets reviewed collectively herein.

So yes—we must exclude discussion about procedure type because it distracts from answering exactly how well—or poorly—it performs *diagnostically* relative to accepted benchmarks defined by expert bodies globally governing antenatal management strategies aimed preventing early deliveries before viability thresholds have reached safely achievable levels without risk outweighing benefit ratios considered acceptable ethically/practically worldwide.

Hence our summary stays focused exclusively on functional limitations tied directly toward identification capacity instead of logistical convenience aspects stemming purely out-of-context comparisons between different types of surveillance tools employed throughout pregnancy stages including late gestation periods approaching full-term milestones closely monitored routinely nowadays thanks largely advances made possible through integration digital health technologies increasingly accessible widely across healthcare systems globally improving access equitably regardless socioeconomic status differences present locally regionally nationally internationally affecting equity outcomes significantly impacting maternal newborn survival trajectories positively overall—but again irrelevant here since focus rests entirely upon ability-to-detect-not-on-access-or-equity-related-issues-per-se.
